metric_value: Born 1947; grew up in Chatham, on Cape Cod, Massachusetts.
Six dining rooms (575 seats), cocktail lounges (200), opera house (300); June-Sept season; over 1,000 diners per night.
Five-star destination.
Approximately 10,000 pieces of paper per evening (six slips per table for 1,000+ diners), all requiring next-day reconciliation.

metric_name: Business control rationale for paper receipts
metric_value: Servers are independent business people who can collect cash and merchandise; paper trails deterred theft.
Age twelve; described as 'Basic Business Controls 101, delivered at age twelve.'
By his third summer at the restaurant, Kastner was promoted to manager of the bookkeeping department.
Adding machines, pencils, and lined accounting workbooks — no path to making the job faster or smarter.
Nearly half a million people employed every night to sort checks through the financial system circa 1960.
notes: Kastner's recollection of a widely cited statistic from the period; exact figure not independently verified in chapter.

metric_value: Every single check was touched by human hands at minimum half a dozen times before being returned to account holder.
Making an airline reservation required a half-hour telephone call and a trip to the travel agent to collect a physical ticket.
Commerce was extraordinarily labor intensive, and almost nobody in 1960 thought there was anything unusual about that.
Enrolled at Cornell's School of Hotel Administration in 1965 expecting to find automation answers there.
First Friday concert: The Beach Boys; second: The Rolling Stones; fourth: Bob Dylan.

metric_name: Hotel School model hotel automation level
metric_value: Front desk ran on an NCR paper-tape cash register; cash procedures nearly identical to Cape Cod restaurant. No plans to change.

metric_name: Hotel Ezra Cornell punch-card automation attempt
metric_value: Upper-class accounting team used donated IBM punch-card tabulating equipment to automate Hotel Ezra Cornell (HEC) weekend books.
Programmed by physical wiring on a plugboard — patching cables like an old telephone switchboard — not software.
Could sort, count, and add 80-column card data but could not perform conditional logic (if-then category assignment) with sufficient flexibility via plugboard wiring.
'Our eyes were bigger than what the technology could deliver.' Team conceded defeat; fell back on paper receipts and human bookkeepers.

metric_name: Decision to take a year off and join MIT
metric_value: Took a year off from Cornell; took a job at MIT as a full-time computer operator to learn computing at the foundation level.
Reader-printer workhorse: ingested punch-card batches, wrote to magnetic tape at 800 bpi, printed mainframe output. 'The loading dock.'
Daytime: timesharing for dozens of simultaneous academic users. Off-peak: batch mode processing jobs queued on tape.
Techniques developed at MIT on the 7094 fed directly into the Multics operating system.
Dedicated development machine with attached graphics workstation and trackball — 'an exotic luxury in 1966.'

metric_name: Longevity of climate code on IBM 360/65
metric_value: Base FORTRAN airflow-analysis code written on the MIT 360/65 was still embedded in climate models as late as the 2020s.

metric_name: Batch processing economic model of 1960s computing
metric_value: Three-machine pipeline (cards in, tape to mainframe, results back) defined the economics of computing for the next decade.
Noisy rooms, constant pressure to keep jobs moving, modest pay — 'factory work in a white coat.'
Taught himself FORTRAN on the company's idle overnight CPU cycles — 'on the company dime.'
Taught himself IBM assembler during quiet overnight hours on the operator's own accounts.
Spacewar! video game appeared permanently installed on the 360/65 graphics workstation; Kastner could play it on a '$5 million mainframe' during overnight shifts.
Helped a cryptic researcher run classified simulations. Researcher said only: 'Things that go boom.'
Developing foundations of timesharing and multiuser computing that would eventually produce the Multics operating system.
Researchers argued about artificial intelligence 'with a fervor that felt more like religion than engineering.'

metric_name: LISP use in AI at MIT
metric_value: LISP used to manipulate symbolic representations of knowledge for AI research circa 1966.
ELIZA had text-based conversations that 'unnerved people who didn't know it had no inner life whatsoever.'
From the operator's console, AI wasn't magic — it was just more code competing for time on the same machines that ran payroll and climate models.
Early DEC PDP/8 minicomputers were appearing in labs around Cambridge in 1966-1967.
The glass-house era — when computing meant a centralized room behind locked doors — was beginning, almost invisibly, to crack.

metric_name: Return to Cornell with new credentials
metric_value: Returned to Cornell in 1967 with dual credential: computer operations and programming experience from MIT.
Only one programming job available; required PL/1 experience. Nobody had it in 1967; job went elsewhere.
Asking for experienced PL/1 programmers in 1967 was 'roughly equivalent to asking for experienced ChatGPT programmers in 2020.'
source_page: Sidebar: Why PL/1 Was a Difficult Ask

metric_value: Designed in mid-1960s to unify FORTRAN's mathematical capability with COBOL's structured data handling — powerful but extremely new in 1967.
source_page: Sidebar: Why PL/1 Was a Difficult Ask

metric_name: Cornell IBM 360/65 operator job and pay
metric_value: Operated Cornell's brand-new IBM 360/65; paid $2.00/hour (federal minimum wage) plus unlimited computer time at no charge.

metric_name: Dave Pulleyn assigns Kastner to Sysgen Day
metric_value: Cornell operations manager Dave Pulleyn relieved to have experienced 360/65 hand; immediately assigned Kastner to Sysgen Day.
Systems generation: computer creates its own operating software from source code and config. Loaded hundreds of punch-card decks in correct sequence; machine compiled itself.

metric_name: IBM OS source code licensing practice in 1960s
metric_value: IBM provided source code for its operating systems to customers in this era — a practice it abandoned in the 1980s as software became a separate business.
Modified IBM OS/360 enhanced with HASP (Houston Automated Spooling Processor) to manage job queue.
Saturday, September 23, 1967: Arlene Larsen wheeled in punch cards and a 2311 disk pack (13 MB — 'enormous storage by the standards of the day'). Sysgen succeeded.
13 megabytes — described as 'an enormous amount of storage by the standards of the day.'
notes: entity_id used as placeholder; no separate entity entry for this hardware component.

metric_value: Cornell originally planned to install an IBM 360/67 timesharing-capable variant. IBM couldn't deliver it.

metric_name: Cornell's RJE workaround for failed 360/67 delivery
metric_value: Remote job entry over a 56 kbps dedicated telephone line from Upson Hall to Langmuir Labs data center.
CDC 1604 at Upson Hall read student card decks and sent them to the IBM 360/65 at Langmuir Labs; returned print-image files.
The 360/65 was, in effect, Cornell's cloud — a concept that would not have a name for another forty years.
Cornell's off-campus Langmuir Labs data center was described as 'a former GE research building.'

metric_name: Halloween thunderstorm crisis at Cornell data center
metric_value: Water dripped onto I/O electronics cabinet during Halloween storm; Kastner chose orderly shutdown over Emergency Shutdown to avoid days of IBM rewiring.
Pulling the Emergency Shutdown switch would physically disable the computer for days while IBM rewired it — avoided by Kastner's orderly shutdown decision.
Wrote FORTRAN jobs for clients and coursework; automated Statistics 101 lab exercise previously requiring hand-cranked mechanical calculators.

metric_name: Statistics grad student as consulting client
metric_value: Grad student demanded explanation for one-sheet printout replacing expected manual computation stack; became a consulting client.
CHASE = Cornell Hotel School Administration Simulation Exercise; let student teams manage a virtual hotel with pricing, staffing, inventory decisions and real financial consequences.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_name: CHESS — first Hotel School real-time program
metric_value: CHESS (Cornell Educational Strategic Simulation) was the first program in the Hotel School to run in real time.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Chase taught Kastner first-year accounting; later recruited him to enhance and debug CHASE on the IBM 360/65.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: FORTRAN's floating-point arithmetic caused simulation books to come out a few cents off — 'driving accountants batty.' Kastner spent weeks finding and fixing each instance.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: FORTRAN uses floating-point arithmetic (scientific approximation). In accounting, every debit must equal every credit to the penny — making FORTRAN a wrong tool for financial software.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Adopted by Intercontinental Hotels, Marriott, Howard Johnson's, and KFC for management training. Hospitality schools worldwide used them for decades.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Robert M. Chase inducted into Cornell Hotel School's Hall of Fame in 2024.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Left Cornell in 1968 with three years programming experience, two years mainframe operations, working knowledge of IBM systems architecture.
Technology's job was to serve human purposes, not to impress other technologists.
Punch-card tabulators could not do conditional logic at scale.
Timesharing was brilliant but fragile.
IBM could not always deliver what it had promised to universities that had planned around it (e.g., Cornell's 360/67).
A system is not just its code. It is its hardware constraints, institutional context, economic incentives, and the human beings who have to live with it every day.
Took application programming job at Philip Hankins Inc. (PHI), described as the largest data center in Boston and a serious software development house.
Kastner set his sights on Boston, which he characterized as 'the center of the computing universe in 1968.'

metric_name: The paper problem — core unsolved challenge
metric_value: The paper problem was still unsolved when Kastner departed Cornell for Boston. He was 'just getting started.'

metric_name: Arlene Larsen role in Cornell Sysgen
metric_value: Systems programmer Arlene Larsen participated in the September 23, 1967 Sysgen Day alongside Kastner; wheeled in punch-card tray.
Annual HEC weekend: students produce and manage a full hotel experience for industry executives — long-running Cornell tradition.
Cards in → tape to mainframe → results back to 1401 → paper out. Kastner trained to operate all three MIT machines.

metric_name: Kastner's MIT roommate worked at Project MAC
metric_value: Roommate worked at MIT's Project MAC, giving Kastner indirect exposure to timesharing and multiuser computing research.
Automated a Statistics 101 lab exercise that had previously required hand-cranked mechanical calculators; handed in a one-sheet printout.
Adopted Robert Chase's hotel-management simulation for management training.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Adopted Robert Chase's hotel-management simulation for management training.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Adopted Robert Chase's restaurant/hotel simulation for management training.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_value: Adopted Robert Chase's Cornell simulation programs for management training.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_name: Kastner's assessment of simulation vs. real automation
metric_value: Simulations were elegant and showed what computers could do for education, but they were not transforming how actual businesses processed their actual transactions.
source_page: Simulation and the First Real Application (CHASE)

metric_name: Cornell student wage for computer operations
metric_value: Federal minimum wage of $2.00/hour applied to all student workers. Kastner interpreted this as $2.00 plus unlimited free computer time.
Kastner references a '$5 million mainframe' in the context of the MIT 360/65 / Spacewar anecdote.
notes: Approximate value cited by Kastner in the context of using the machine to play Spacewar.

metric_value: Moving ten thousand pieces of paper around was neither a scalable economic activity nor one that seemed likely to help humanity achieve a higher level. He wanted machines to handle the paper.
Parents purchased an old sea captain's house with a small attached restaurant; over six years it expanded to 575 dining seats, 200 lounge seats, and a 300-seat opera house.
Seasonal operation: June to September.
From a bookkeeping standpoint: 'an absolute disaster.'
Cannot get a drink at the bar without a paper slip tied back to the customer's check. Table for two could generate six separate pieces of paper.

metric_name: Kastner's role: matching checks to receipts
metric_value: Spent summers matching checks to receipts, hunting discrepancies, and balancing cashier deposits to the penny.

metric_name: Project MAC significance to computing history
metric_value: Project MAC developed foundations of timesharing and multiuser computing that produced Multics; researchers argued about AI with 'religious fervor.'
Submit a job and wait defined the economics of computing for the next decade after 1966.
Rooms were noisy; constant pressure to keep jobs moving through the pipeline; pay was modest.
Multics operating system techniques, developed at MIT on the 7094, resurfaced in Kastner's career a decade later at Stratus Computer.

metric_name: HEC punch-card automation: design vs. implementation gap
metric_value: Several months of effort produced an elegant design and an infeasible implementation — the tabulator could not execute required conditional logic.
IBM could not deliver the promised 360/67 to Cornell; university scrambled and pivoted to RJE workaround.
See companion study kastner-technology-breadth-memoir-2026 for quantified 47-year coverage breadth (2,537 distinct technologies, 14 thematic rollups).
